# gh-sr inner-container DNS (baked into the container runner image).
#
# The inner dockerd's default bridge is pinned to 10.200.0.1/24 (see daemon.json),
# so the bridge gateway IP is deterministic. dnsmasq listens on that gateway and:
#   * answers host.docker.internal with the bridge gateway IP so AWF agent /
#     bridge containers reach the MCP gateway (which gh-aw runs on the runner
#     container's host network), and
#   * forwards everything else to the upstream resolvers that entrypoint.sh writes
#     to /etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr-upstream.conf at startup.
#
# WHY entrypoint repoints the runner's resolv.conf at this dnsmasq — and why that
# matters for the agent sandbox:
#   gh-aw's firewall (AWF) auto-detects the agent sandbox's DNS servers by parsing the
#   runner container's /etc/resolv.conf, then runs the agent with those resolvers. If
#   the runner inherits the OUTER host resolver (e.g. 172.17.0.1), the sandbox resolves
#   host.docker.internal there and intermittently gets a NON-exempt IP. AWF's sandbox
#   iptables only exempts the inner-bridge gateway from the transparent Squid redirect,
#   so a non-exempt answer force-proxies the MCP gateway POST into Squid, which rejects
#   the origin-form request (ERR_INVALID_URL → HTTP 400) and the agent reports
#   "MCP server(s) failed to launch". Pointing resolv.conf at this dnsmasq makes
#   host.docker.internal resolve authoritatively to the exempt gateway for the runner
#   AND the sandbox, removing the flaky dependency on the outer resolver.
#
# `no-resolv` is REQUIRED: because the runner's /etc/resolv.conf is repointed back at
# this dnsmasq, dnsmasq must NOT read it for upstreams (that would be a resolution
# loop). Upstreams therefore come solely from the generated drop-in above, which
# entrypoint seeds from the runner container's ORIGINAL resolvers (preserving
# enterprise/VPC DNS) or public DNS as a fallback.
#
# Why 10.200.0.1 and NOT 172.17.0.1: the outer runner container itself sits on the
# host's default Docker bridge (172.17.0.0/16, gateway 172.17.0.1). Pinning the inner
# bridge to 172.17.0.x would duplicate the container's own default-gateway IP and
# overlap its eth0 subnet, silently black-holing the container's outbound traffic into
# the inner bridge (host network fine, but every connection inside the runner crawls /
# times out). 10.200.0.0/24 is outside Docker's default address pools (172.16/12 and
# 192.168/16) and the default bridge, so it cannot collide with the outer container's
# network or any auto-allocated inner network.
#
# Baking this (instead of detecting docker0 at runtime and restarting dockerd)
# lets the inner dockerd start exactly once with a stable configuration.
address=/host.docker.internal/10.200.0.1
listen-address=10.200.0.1
bind-dynamic

input.old_string:
# ── 3. host.docker.internal via dnsmasq (baked config) ──────────────────────────
# gh-aw agent containers reach the MCP gateway via http://host.docker.internal:<port>.
# dnsmasq (config baked at build time) listens on the pinned bridge gateway 10.200.0.1
# and answers host.docker.internal there; daemon.json already points inner containers'
# DNS at 10.200.0.1. dockerd is NOT restarted.
echo "[entrypoint] starting dnsmasq..."
if pgrep -x dnsmasq &>/dev/null; then
    kill -HUP "$(pgrep -x dnsmasq)" 2>/dev/null || true
else
    dnsmasq --conf-dir=/etc/dnsmasq.d 2>/dev/null || \
        echo "[entrypoint] WARNING: dnsmasq failed to start; host.docker.internal may not resolve"

input.new_string:
# ── 3. host.docker.internal via dnsmasq (baked config + runtime upstreams) ──────
# gh-aw agent containers reach the MCP gateway via http://host.docker.internal:<port>.
# dnsmasq (config baked at build time) listens on the pinned bridge gateway and answers
# host.docker.internal there; daemon.json already points inner containers' DNS at the
# same gateway. dockerd is NOT restarted.
#
# Upstreams: the baked config sets `no-resolv`, so dnsmasq forwards non-local queries
# ONLY to the servers written here. We seed them from the runner container's ORIGINAL
# /etc/resolv.conf (captured before the repoint below) so enterprise/VPC DNS keeps
# working, dropping loopback stubs and the gateway itself, and fall back to public
# resolvers when nothing usable remains. This MUST run before the resolv.conf repoint.
UPSTREAM_NS=()
while read -r _ns; do
    case "${_ns}" in
        127.*|::1|"${BRIDGE_GW}"|"") continue ;;
        *) UPSTREAM_NS+=("${_ns}") ;;
    esac
done < <(awk '/^nameserver/ {print $2}' /etc/resolv.conf 2>/dev/null)
if [ "${#UPSTREAM_NS[@]}" -eq 0 ]; then
    UPSTREAM_NS=(8.8.8.8 1.1.1.1)
fi
{
    echo "# Generated by entrypoint.sh at startup — dnsmasq upstream resolvers."
    echo "# Seeded from the runner container's original /etc/resolv.conf (or public DNS"
    echo "# fallback). Kept separate from the baked gh-sr.conf so host.docker.internal"
    echo "# stays authoritative while upstream DNS matches the host environment."
    for _ns in "${UPSTREAM_NS[@]}"; do echo "server=${_ns}"; done
} > /etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr-upstream.conf

echo "[entrypoint] starting dnsmasq (upstreams: ${UPSTREAM_NS[*]})..."
if pgrep -x dnsmasq &>/dev/null; then
    kill -HUP "$(pgrep -x dnsmasq)" 2>/dev/null || true
else
    dnsmasq --conf-dir=/etc/dnsmasq.d 2>/dev/null || \
        echo "[entrypoint] WARNING: dnsmasq failed to start; host.docker.internal may not resolve"
fi

# Repoint the runner container's OWN resolver at the bundled dnsmasq. This is the crux
# of the host.docker.internal reliability fix: gh-aw's firewall auto-detects the agent
# sandbox's DNS from this very /etc/resolv.conf, so making dnsmasq (authoritative for
# host.docker.internal → the AWF-exempt bridge gateway) the sole resolver stops the
# sandbox from falling back to the outer host resolver and mapping host.docker.internal
# to a non-exempt IP — the intermittent failure that force-proxied the MCP gateway POST
# into Squid (ERR_INVALID_URL → "MCP server(s) failed to launch").
#
# Wait until dnsmasq actually answers on the gateway before repointing, so the runner's
# own resolution (config.sh registration, image pulls) is never briefly broken. `dig`
# ships with the image (dnsutils) and @<gw> bypasses resolv.conf during the probe.
for _i in $(seq 1 20); do
    if dig +short +time=1 +tries=1 host.docker.internal @"${BRIDGE_GW}" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then
        break
    fi
    sleep 0.25
done
# /etc/resolv.conf is a Docker-managed bind mount: rewrite its CONTENTS in place (never
# rename). On the rare read-only mount we fall back to the inherited resolver (degraded
# but functional) rather than aborting the runner.
if printf 'nameserver %s\n' "${BRIDGE_GW}" > /etc/resolv.conf 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "[entrypoint] /etc/resolv.conf now points at dnsmasq (${BRIDGE_GW})"
else
    echo "[entrypoint] WARNING: could not rewrite /etc/resolv.conf; agent sandbox may inherit the host resolver"
fi

content.text:
I've traced the intermittent failure end-to-end and fixed it. Here's the summary.

## Root cause

The agentic runner builds a sandbox around the agent using gh-aw-firewall (AWF): all egress on ports 80/443/8080 is transparently DNAT'd into a Squid proxy, **except** traffic to the inner Docker bridge gateway (`10.200.0.1`), which AWF's sandbox iptables explicitly exempts. The agent reaches the MCP gateway at `http://host.docker.internal:8080`, so `host.docker.internal` **must** resolve to that exempt gateway IP.

The bug: AWF auto-detects the sandbox's DNS servers by parsing the **runner container's `/etc/resolv.conf`** (confirmed via [gh-aw-firewall PR #1513](https://github.com/github/gh-aw-firewall/pull/1513)). gh-sr created the runner container without `--dns`, so that file pointed at the **outer host** resolver (`172.17.0.1`) — never gh-sr's authoritative dnsmasq at `10.200.0.1`. So `host.docker.internal` resolution depended on a non-authoritative resolver:

- Sometimes the sandbox's `/etc/hosts` entry (`→ 10.200.0.1`) won the lookup → exempt IP → **works**.
- Intermittently the agent's resolver (Node's c-ares path bypasses `/etc/hosts`) queried `172.17.0.1`, got a **non-exempt** IP back → the gateway POST was force-proxied into Squid → Squid rejected the origin-form request with `ERR_INVALID_URL` (HTTP 400) → Claude reported both MCP servers `failed` → `ERR_API: MCP server(s) failed to launch`.

The evidence chain: failed-run `agent-stdio.log` showed the Squid `ERR_INVALID_URL` page; `iptables-audit.txt` showed the `:8080` DNAT-to-Squid with `RETURN` exemptions only for `10.200.0.1`/`172.30.0.1`; `docker-compose.redacted.yml` showed `dns: [172.17.0.1, 8.8.8.8]`. The successful run resolved via `/etc/hosts` and connected cleanly.

## The fix

Make gh-sr's authoritative dnsmasq the **only** resolver the runner (and therefore the auto-detected sandbox) uses, so `host.docker.internal` always lands on the exempt gateway:

- `internal/runner/agentic-runner-image/entrypoint.sh` — after dnsmasq is confirmed answering, repoint the runner's `/etc/resolv.conf` to a single `nameserver <bridge-gw>`. dnsmasq answers `host.docker.internal` authoritatively (→ exempt IP) and forwards everything else to the runner's **original** upstream resolvers (captured into a generated `/etc/dnsmasq.d/gh-sr-upstream.conf`), so enterprise/VPC DNS still works. This closes both the `/etc/hosts`-vs-DNS race and the DNS-fallback hole.
- `internal/runner/agentic-runner-image/dnsmasq-gh-sr.conf` — add `no-resolv` (prevents a resolution loop now that resolv.conf points back at dnsmasq) and move upstreams to the runtime drop-in.
- `internal/agentic/agentic.go` + `internal/doctor/doctor.go` — new `ValidateContainerInnerResolv` check (`container-inner-resolv`) so `gh sr doctor` flags a stale/unpinned resolv.conf before a job hits the bug, with a unit test.
- Docs updated (image internals, doctor checklist, troubleshooting row).

This also respects the existing guardrails: single `dockerd` start preserved, no `daemon.json` post-start rewrite, no collision with the `172.17.0.0/16` host bridge, and the collision-avoidance fallback gateway is honored throughout.

## Verification

`go build ./...`, `go vet`, and the full `go test ./...` suite all pass; `bash -n` and `shellcheck` pass on the entrypoint. The change bumps the image layout revision, so the runners need a rebuild to pick it up:

```bash
gh sr rebuild <agentic-runner>
gh sr doctor --host <host>   # expect: resolv.conf pinned to dnsmasq
```

One deployment note: this fix only takes effect after the image is rebuilt — existing running containers keep the old behavior until `gh sr rebuild`. Since I can't reproduce the intermittent failure locally, I'd suggest rebuilding and re-running the workflow that was flaking a few times to confirm the MCP launch is now stable.
